name: Accidental coupling through shared database access and circular dependencies

planted_issues.description: OrderService directly accesses UserService._db_session (a class-level private attribute) to query the users database (line 65). This bypasses UserService's public API, creates tight coupling to its internal database structure, and means changes to UserService's schema silently break OrderService.

Both OrderService (via its own db) and BillingService (via a new DatabaseSession to the orders DB on lines 118-123) write to the `orders` table. Additionally, NotificationService writes to the orders table (lines 167-170). Three services mutate the same table with no ownership, no locking, and no coordination — causing potential data races and conflicting updates.

Circular dependency chain: OrderService.create_order creates BillingService and calls charge_order (line 96), which creates NotificationService and calls send_payment_confirmation (line 127), which creates OrderService and writes to the orders table (lines 166-170). This circular call chain makes the system untestable, creates infinite recursion risk, and prevents independent deployment.

clean_areas.description: NotificationService.send() has a well-defined public API with clear docstring, proper argument documentation, return type, user validation, and structured logging.
